text: For context: what you’re seeing is rot. If the leaves were buried into the soil, it’s possible but unlikely just the leaves are rotting from contact with wet soil. More commonly (or if the leaves aren’t in contact with the soil) it means you’ve got very advanced root rot that has spread into the stem and is now working its way up the plant. Anything below the rot line is already toast. You might be able to propagate by removing whatever stem is still viable and rooting that.

text:
This is tip burn from minerals in your tap water. Spider plants are notoriously sensitive to fluoride and chlorine, way more than most houseplants, which is why your other plants are fine with the same soil and water. A year of weekly tap water leaves salt deposits in the soil that burn the leaf tips from the inside out.

Switch to filtered water, distilled, or even just tap water that's sat out for 24 hours. Run a good flush through the soil every few weeks to clear the buildup. On the babies, spider plants usually need to be slightly rootbound and get enough bright indirect light before they send out runners. If it's in the corner of the room, it might not be getting quite enough direct light to trigger that.
label: recommendation

text:
your issue isn't the water. 99% of the time, it's not the water. the whole 'mineral sensitivity' is mostly a scapegoat for folks who can't blame themselves for miswatering, so they blame water chemistry. when they themselves know next to nothing about water chemistry, which chemicals/minerals *might* be an issue, or how those chemicals interact w/ OTHER chemicals present. or what's actually in their tap to begin with. they don't bother to even test, but just blame the water. silly humans.

to even begin to blame the water before even knowing what your water chemistry actually is, as some here are doing, is simply foolishness.

my wager is you're not letting the pot dry enough waay inside the soil column. that and mites have been the only two causes i've ever had that verifiably cause brown tipping, and when addressed, have always cleared it up.

i've used tap in nearly every region of the u.s., AND in a small area of the mideast. always kept spider plants. NEVER had an actual water chemistry issue for plants, both terrestrial and aquatic. (including spider plants).

i'll wager that anyone here making chemistry/mineral claims has never once sent their water for a detailed assay (icp analysis) for whatever scapegoat minerals they're trying to blame.

it's the waterer, not the water.

text:
yellowing leaves don't always mean overwatering! severe underwatering will cause this too.

when a pothos goes 28 days without water, it goes into survival mode. it will purposely sacrifice and kill off its oldest leaves (the ones closest to the soil, which are the yellow ones in your photo) to preserve moisture for the growing vines.

also, after a month of drought, your soil has probably become hydrophobic (it repels water like a dry sponge). bottom watering might not even be reaching the roots right now. i’d recommend giving it a massive, thorough top-watering in the sink to fully reset the soil!
label: explanation

I’ll be honest, throw it away.

Even a small infestation is a pain in the ass to deal with. I recently had one, and I’m still proper annoyed. Heck, I sprayed a small jade plant with rubbing alcohol, then let it soak in a bowl of warm water with Dawn, and rubbing alcohol. I quarantined the plant, then two days later I found a small mealie bug on one of the leaves. HOW?? I threw away a bunch of props. I cut this jade to free it from its roots, soaked it again, and will be repotting (we’ll see if it lives 🤦🏻‍♀️). I checked all my plants. Cleaned everything. Threw away all the soil the plants got near.

Mealies move in such random ways. So far there’s none on the plants right next to the jade and props. But, there were two small mealies on one of my alocasia props, on a shelf above the jade 🤷🏻‍♀️

text:
This could be a bit unpopular but if you want to recover the plant, you could do a few rounds - remove, monitor (and remove), remove again (because it's hard to remove everything the first time) and then apply a preventative:

take several large size alcohol wipes (not the small dabby kind but the hankerchief sized ones), wipe off all the white stuff you see. Wipe down the stems top to bottom, the tops and bottoms of every leaf, do several once-overs. You need isopropyl because water may make removal harder (they stick).

you can also try spraying down with mild soapy water (one or two drops of dishwasher liquid to 400ml of water), then use a rag and wipe all of it off. This is probably the tedious part but based on the plant size, it's still doable. The part that takes the most time is finding the hidden white crap, which is probably stuck all over.

would recommend that you then come back to this plant a few hours later and check again, and again for the next few days. Just wipe down every time. Some may come back too, just keep wiping/removing. There are different stages to a mealybug life cycle, so you may not have completely gotten everything on day 1. Certain stages aren't as destroyable by soap sprays and so on.

After a few rounds of this, you could also do an insecticide soak of the soil (look up recommendations for any brand that works for mealy bugs), and then spray the leaves too.

Keep monitoring and wiping down/ removing.

Apply to other infected plants.

Other things to look out for:

- often times, its the ants that bring mealybugs and stick them on plants. if you want to, locate and remove ants on the plants, use borax traps etc. Because they will keep trying to bring the mealybugs back - if they successfully achieved such a big infestation the first time, this is their big score, so it takes repeated work to remove.

- it's really whether or not you want to invest the time, and even then a successful result doesn't mean they won't come back. This might be a higher level of attention that you're not used to giving to just one plant. So if you have a lot of plants or not a lot of time, that's why people are saying to throw it away.
label: recommendation

text:
Mealybugs!

I actually think these are one of the easier pests to deal with - and easier to see so more satisfying to kill 😅

And you can better gauge your progress / feel good when they are eradicated. Plus treating for these will take care of any mite / help be preventive for thrip issues you might have starting that you can’t see yet.

It just takes some dedicated attention from you for an afternoon (or a few afternoons depending on how many plants and how much time you can spend treating every leaf individually).

Disposable gloves, roll of paper towels, couple bottles of 70% isopropyl alcohol (or a big jug of it if you have it!), water, and garbage bag (or gallon bag that you seal and then throw in garbage bag and seal).

You will be rubbing down every single leaf, top and bottom, nooks and crannies (add q-tips if/where needed) of your infested plants with paper towels soaked in the isopropyl alcohol and water - I’ve seen different ratios recommended, I’ve seen people do straight alcohol. You know your plants best so can test out what works on a leaf and adjust as needed to dilute with more water or to use a stronger amount of alcohol.

Keep switching up to fresh paper towels doused with alcohol and discard the used paper towels between every few leaves. Seal away mealy bug wiped paper towels in gallon bags or trash bags. The alcohol should kill them but you could be wiping off microscopic eggs for any houseplant pests that survive too so that’s why you’re sealing them away!

You can also use castille soap in water mix if you don’t have alcohol on hand or fear alcohol will be too much for all your plants.

Always switch to new paper towels and change gloves entirely between plants.

And make sure to do this somewhere away from windows in your home or outside in the shade where the leaves of your plants are not going to have sun shining on them while you are treating them!

Then a weekly follow up to make sure you’ve got them all and a new infestation can’t break out because of a missed couple of eggs.

Captain Jack’s Spinosad 0.005% spray is also good - just make sure to do it out of the sun, drench the underside of the leaves, and if you have a higher concentration in the bottle you buy triple check and ensure that you are diluting it (I have seen some horror stories here - plants people hit with 0.05% or even in one case 0.5% and that poor person’s plant leaves looked like they’d been transformed into the surface of the moon)
label: recommendation

They are not really conducive to being held in a pot unless you are constantly pruning and shaping. You can prune off the long stems and plant them right back into the pot to make it look full. This plant is very forgiving and responds really well to pruning.

text:
Thrips!!!! I just had them on some of my plants. I won’t lie, I caught it much earlier than you did so what I did might not work, but here’s how I treated mine.

I washed & repotted all affected or suspected plants and any plants that were nearby or in contact with any infected or suspected specimens. I used the neem oil fungicide spray you can get from stores (in the US at least) as a first defense but didn’t feel like it did anything. About a week later I applied beneficial mites (sf nematodes and swirskii) to all plants in my collection. These worked tremendously well and I had a lot of fun watching the mites patrol the plants. They gave me a lot of mental security too lol.

I’m thrip free at this point and all my plants have recovered. I highly recommend beneficials but a lot of ppl on here prefer systemic pesticides. You do have to choose one or the other (systemics kill the beneficials) and that will depend on your set up and what you are comfortable with :)
label: experience_sharing

label: explanation
notes: this comment contains information seeking, explanation, and experience sharing. However, the intended purpose of the experience sharing seems to be to support the explanation, that the variety of blueberry affects when they finish. The question "do you know what kind of blackberry bush it is" is asked, but the commenter doesn't seem to really want the answer. They just want the commenter to be aware that this can be a factor.  Therefore, the label I selected is explanation.

text: I can only keep my cat of of my plants if I give her something to distract her. When she was a kitten she would dig through my plants at night, until I bought her enough toys to entertain her all night (now she thankfully sleeps at night). She would munch on my monstera until I bought cat wheat for her to eat, etc. So my advice would be to buy something that will get your cat out of the plant, maybe a cat bed that the cat knows it can lay in all the time without any consequences. Good luck

text:
Holy hell that's an impressive snake plant!

Does the cat have any textures or materials (s)he hates? If yes, try putting that on soil. Mine, for example, hates the sound of plastic bags so if i had your problem i'd wrap a bag around the plants so it covers the soil and she'd stay away.

Edit: you could also stick some skewers in there without damaging the plant
label: recommendation
notes: Commenter asks a question, but isn't really expecting an answer. It's just to get the poster to think about textures their cat might hate.

text:
Beautiful photo beautiful plant beautiful cat. I have felt your pain. Tin foil resolved my issue.

Crumple up some tinfoil loosely and stick it in the plant pot. Don’t have to wedge it in there tight... just sort of crinkle-wrap it around the base of your plant stems and leave it in there, set on the top of the soil. My cat hate tinfoil. This immediately stopped them digging in the plant pots I didn’t want them in.
label: recommendation
notes: Commenter shares their experience, but they're using it to support their recommendation. Primarily, the commenter is telling the poster what to do.
